topic: Data Preprocessing: Handling Outliers, Missing Values, and Skewed Data

questions.scenario: A fintech corporation is deploying an online, real-time credit card fraud detection system designed to handle up to 15,000 transactions per second (TPS) with a strict sub-15ms end-to-end latency SLA. During initial profiling, you find that the feature 'historical_merchant_risk_score' is missing for 8% of transactions (Missing at Random, MAR), and the 'transaction_amount' feature is heavily right-skewed (skewness > 5.2). The downstream classifier is a latency-sensitive logistic regression model.
questions.question: Which combination of preprocessing steps optimizes predictive performance while consistently meeting the sub-15ms real-time latency SLA?
options: A) Apply an iterative Multivariate Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE) with a Random Forest regressor for the missing scores, and a Yeo-Johnson power transform for the transaction amount.
B) Apply a K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) Imputer (K=5) optimized with a KD-Tree spatial index for missing values, and a Box-Cox transformation (with a shift parameter) for the transaction amount.
C) Use simple median imputation coupled with an binary 'is_missing' indicator column for the missing scores, and a computationally cheap Log1p (log(x + 1)) transformation for the transaction amount.
D) Utilize a Matrix Factorization (SoftImpute) model to complete the missing data matrix in batches, and a QuantileTransformer (mapping to a normal distribution) for the transaction amount.
questions.correct_answer_text: C) Use simple median imputation coupled with an binary 'is_missing' indicator column for the missing scores, and a computationally cheap Log1p (log(x + 1)) transformation for the transaction amount.
questions.explanation: In high-throughput, real-time production pipelines (15,000 TPS, <15ms SLA), algorithmic complexity during inference is the primary constraint. MICE, KNN Imputation, and Matrix Factorization require significant computational overhead at inference time (MICE runs iterative models, KNN requires computing distances against the training set which is O(N*D) or O(D*log N) with KD-Tree). Simple median imputation has O(1) time complexity. Adding a missingness indicator retains the information that the value was missing (which is crucial for MAR data). Similarly, Log1p is a mathematically simple, element-wise O(1) operation that handles right-skewed data effectively, whereas Yeo-Johnson and Box-Cox require parameter estimation and complex exponentiation operations that add non-trivial overhead to the serving path.

questions.scenario: You are designing an outlier detection pipeline for a high-dimensional IoT sensor network deployed on gas turbines. The dataset contains 320 continuous features representing high-frequency vibrational and thermal telemetry. Outliers represent structural anomalies and constitute less than 0.05% of the dataset. The system must run on an edge gateway with severe memory constraints (maximum 512MB RAM allocated to the container) but must capture complex, non-linear interactions without assuming a normal distribution.
questions.question: Which anomaly detection algorithm should you select to best balance memory footprint, high-dimensional scaling, and non-parametric outlier detection?
options: A) Local Outlier Factor (LOF) using a Mahalanobis distance metric to account for feature correlations.
B) Isolation Forest (iForest) configured with feature sub-spacing (max_features = 0.2) and a shallow tree depth constraint.
C) One-Class Support Vector Machine (OC-SVM) utilizing a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel with hyperparameter tuning on the full dataset.
D) Minimum Covariance Determinant (MCD) estimator coupled with Mahalanobis distance analysis.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) Isolation Forest (iForest) configured with feature sub-spacing (max_features = 0.2) and a shallow tree depth constraint.
questions.explanation: Isolation Forest (iForest) is highly suitable for high-dimensional, memory-constrained environments. By isolating anomalies via random partitioning, it has a linear time complexity O(n) and a very low memory footprint since it only needs to store a forest of extremely shallow trees. Feature sub-spacing (max_features=0.2) directly addresses the curse of dimensionality and reduces memory usage further. In contrast, Local Outlier Factor (LOF) and OC-SVM scale poorly with sample size and feature count; LOF requires storing and querying pairwise distance matrices (O(n^2) space complexity), and OC-SVM requires solving a quadratic programming problem that scales quadratically with the number of samples. MCD requires inverting a 320x320 covariance matrix, which becomes highly unstable and computationally expensive when features are collinear or highly dimensional.

questions.scenario: A data scientist is building a pipeline to predict house prices. The target variable 'SalePrice' is highly right-skewed. The pipeline uses a Gradient Boosting Regressor (GBDT). To handle the skewness of the target, the data scientist writes a script that applies a log-transformation to the target variable of the entire dataset before conducting a 5-fold cross-validation. They evaluate the model using Mean Squared Error (MSE) computed on the log-transformed predictions against the log-transformed targets.
questions.question: What critical methodological flaw and performance risk are introduced by this preprocessing and evaluation setup?
options: A) Data leakage is introduced because the parameters of the log-transform are calculated globally across both training and validation folds.
B) GBDT algorithms cannot handle monotonic target transformations, which will cause the tree splitting criteria to fail.

C) Evaluating with MSE on log-transformed targets implicitly optimizes for Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) rather than standard additive error, potentially leading to massive, unpenalized absolute errors on high-value properties when back-transformed.
D) The log-transformation will compress the variance of the target variable to the point where the GBDT will suffer from severe underfitting due to gradient vanishing.
questions.correct_answer_text: C) Evaluating with MSE on log-transformed targets implicitly optimizes for Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) rather than standard additive error, potentially leading to massive, unpenalized absolute errors on high-value properties when back-transformed.
questions.explanation: While log-transforming the target variable is highly effective for normalizing right-skewed distributions, calculating MSE in the log-space changes the loss function's sensitivity. Minimizing MSE in the log-space is mathematically closer to minimizing relative error (percentage error) in the original scale. Consequently, when predictions are back-transformed ($e^y$), the model will tolerate massive absolute dollar-amount errors on expensive houses because a 10% error on a $2,000,000 home ($200,000) is penalized identically to a 10% error on a $100,000 home ($10,000). Option A is incorrect because log-transformation ($y' = \ln(y)$) is an element-wise mathematical operation that does not depend on dataset-wide parameters (unlike standardization or MinMax scaling), so it does not introduce data leakage. GBDTs are perfectly fine with monotonic target transformations.
